Content Audit — Full Findings

Doctronic.ai Content Audit

10,194 live articles analyzed against GSC and GA4 (Dec 2025 – Jun 2026). Where the traffic comes from, what changed in May and June, and where the cleanup opportunities are — across both EmberTribe-produced and Doctronic-produced content.

Articles Live

282K
↓ 40% from April peak · 2nd straight monthly decline
June Blog Clicks (GSC)

↓ 42% vs May · ET 85K · DT 83K
Declining Articles
619
>35% drop May→Jun · was 91 a month ago
June update (data through June 30): no recovery. Blog search clicks fell another 42% in June (305K → 176K) and daily clicks sat flat at ~7.6K/day for the entire month — 45% below the pre-drop rate. Articles down >35% grew from 91 to 619. Site-wide GA4 shows a record 867K sessions, but that growth is on Doctronic’s product pages, not the audited blog content — the split is broken out on the next slide.
EMBERTRIBEDoctronic.ai · Updated July 3, 2026 with full June data · GSC + GA4 service account data

Traffic Trajectory

Organic Traffic Growth (GA4)

Doctronic grew ~10x from December 2025 to the April 2026 peak. May pulled back 20% — and in June the audited blog content fell another 16% even as site-wide sessions set a record. The two lines are now telling opposite stories, so both are shown below.

Monthly Organic Sessions — doctronic.ai
Organic Search channel only · GA4 property 373721890 · blog landing-page split available from April 2026
Read: the blue bars look healthy — June site-wide organic sessions hit a record 867K. But the content this audit covers (red bars) fell again: blog sessions went Apr 467K → May 337K → Jun 282K, now 40% below peak, and GSC blog clicks fell 42% month-over-month. June’s surge landed on Doctronic’s product surfaces (/questions pages and the homepage), with session engagement dropping from 75% to ~61% — and most of it is not visible as clicks in Search Console, consistent with AI-surface referrals rather than a recovery of blog rankings.
Why this is urgent: the blog program has now declined two months in a row with zero recovery days in the daily data. At the current run-rate the domain gives up roughly 130K blog clicks per month versus the pre-drop baseline, and the refresh/consolidation work on the Action Plan slide only gets more expensive the longer the depressed baseline holds.
EMBERTRIBEDoctronic.ai Content Audit

Production Comparison

Nearly Equal Clicks From a Third of the Volume

In June, EmberTribe articles drove 50.5% of classified blog clicks with 28% of the article count — the efficiency gap held straight through the algorithmic drop (both streams fell ~42–43% in June). Read with the caveats below.

🔥 EmberTribe (ET)
Articles Live2,876
Articles Ranked1,464 (51%)
June Clicks85,000
June Impressions17.1M
Avg Position9.3
Clicks / Article29.6
🏥 Doctronic Own (DT)
Articles Live7,318
Articles Ranked2,248 (31%)
June Clicks83,461
June Impressions19.5M
Avg Position9.3
Clicks / Article11.4
Caveats before leaning on this: the two cohorts differ in age — maturity affects click accumulation, and we have not isolated the impact of any backlink campaigns on either set. Read 29.6 vs 11.4 as directional evidence of efficiency, not a controlled like-for-like comparison. Ranked-article counts here and on the next slide are from the May 31 audit snapshot; click and impression figures are June 1–30. The ranked-rate gap remains the more durable signal.
Click Share by Source
June 1–30 · 168.5K classified blog clicks (excludes 7.7K clicks on pages newer than the audit inventory)

Indexation Efficiency

Ranked vs. Unranked — Diminishing Returns on Volume

51% of EmberTribe articles have GSC impressions vs. 31% of Doctronic’s own. Some of that gap is indexing lag on newer content, but at 7,318 articles the volume-first approach is showing decreasing marginal returns. (Counts on this slide are the May 31 audit snapshot.)

Articles Ranked vs. Unranked
Ranked = any GSC impressions in the last 30 days
What this says: 5,070 of Doctronic’s 7,318 articles (69%) are invisible in search. Even granting generous indexing lag for recent publishes, thousands of older DT articles have never earned impressions.
Fair caveat: 1,141 of ET’s unranked articles are from the April–May spring campaign and are still inside the normal 60–90 day indexing window. The ET ranked rate should improve from here; the DT backlog is largely past that window.
Implication: each additional article published into the current structure earns less than the last. Consolidation and quality (next slides) matter more than added volume right now.

Traffic Dip Diagnosis

June Confirms It: No Recovery From the May 22 Drop

What looked like a one-week cliff in May is now the operating baseline. Daily GSC clicks broke on May 22 and spent the entire month of June flat at ~7.6K/day — 45% below the pre-drop rate, with zero recovery days. June blog clicks finished at 176K vs 305K in May (−42%). Articles down more than 35% grew from 91 to 619, split 287 EmberTribe / 332 Doctronic — both streams fell at nearly identical rates (−42% / −43%), confirming a domain-level algorithmic event rather than a problem isolated to either production source.

Daily Clicks — May 1 to June 30, 2026 (GSC)
Green = pre-drop · Red = May 22 onward. Note: monthly figures elsewhere in this report are GA4 sessions; this chart is GSC clicks — the two metrics differ in scale but tell the same story.
Run-Rate Break
−45%
13.8K clicks/day May 1–21 → 7.6K/day average across all of June — 30 straight days, no recovery
Impressions Halved
3.0M→1.6M
Daily impressions halved on May 22 and stayed there through June 30 — rankings fell out of view, not just CTR
Position Drift
9.0→10.4
June average vs 9.0 pre-drop, with late-June spikes to 12–13 — positions are drifting further out, not recovering
No Manual Action
Algo
GSC shows no manual penalty — this is algorithmic devaluation, which is recoverable
Brand Held
Safe
“doctronic ai” holds pos 1.0 and “ai doctor” clicks grew 8.2K→9.8K in June — the product is fine, the blog is under scrutiny
Decline Source Split
Of 619 declining articles — roughly proportional to each stream’s ranked footprint
Top 15 Declining Articles — Click Loss
Prior (May 1–31) vs Recent (June 1–30)
What Survived, Gained — or Swapped
Monthly clicks, May vs June
PageMayJuneChange
retatrutide-dosage-chart-and-dosing-schedule1,2482,078+67%
foundayo-weight-loss-results723840+16%
semaglutide-to-tirzepatide conversion2,3822,296−4%
herpes-cure-2025-update2,6862,145−20%
how-to-induce-vomiting-safely4,4922,323−48%
retatrutide-dosage-chart-how-dosing-works5,017982−80%
The survival pattern still holds — with one twist: utility formats (dose-conversion guides, results trackers) held or grew through June while generic advice kept falling. The twist: Google swapped winners inside the retatrutide cannibalization cluster — the original dosage-chart article fell 80% while its internal duplicate grew 67%. The traffic did not leave the domain; it moved to the duplicate. That instability is exactly what the consolidation plan eliminates.
Why it matters for the buckets: when refreshing the 619 declining articles and the 1,987 opportunity-zone articles, converting generic-advice formats into chart/Q&A/utility formats follows the proven survivor pattern instead of guessing.
What we can and cannot say: declines cluster around GLP-1 content (seasonal hype normalizing), medication-timing queries (likely YMYL algorithm shift), and viral health queries (natural decay). Because ET and DT articles declined at comparable rates in both May and June (−42% vs −43% in June), the data does not support attributing the drop to either side’s publishing practices — the recovery plan applies to the whole domain, and June proves it will not fix itself.
#SrcSlugPrior ClicksRecent ClicksDropLikely Driver

Self-Competition

Cannibalization Is a Site-Wide Pattern

505 queries have two or more Doctronic pages competing against each other, splitting impressions and suppressing rankings (analysis window Mar 30–May 29; the June retatrutide winner-swap below shows the pattern is still active). The estimated cost is ~128K clicks/month. Who wrote which page matters less than the fix: consolidate duplicates, redirect the losers, and adopt a shared keyword map so it stops happening.

Total Groups
505
Queries with 2+ pages on the domain competing against each other
High Severity
13
Groups with 20K+ impressions being split — fix these first
Est. Monthly Loss
128K
Clicks lost to self-competition across the domain
Severity Distribution
High = 20K+ impressions split, Medium = 5K+
Top Conflicts by Split Impressions
Top 10 queries where competing pages split the most visibility
⚠ Retatrutide Split

Two articles split 49K impressions for “retatrutide dosage calculator”. June update: Google swapped winners inside this cluster — the original page fell 80% while the competing duplicate grew 67%. The merge is now urgent, not optional.

⚠ Vomiting Content Split

Two near-identical ET articles split 74K combined impressions. One consolidated page would likely rank top 3.

⚠ Drug Cluster Duplication

Both streams generated multiple pages per drug. Zepbound has 6 competing ET pages; gabapentin and prednisone have mixed ET/DT overlaps. A shared keyword map prevents this going forward.

Action Plan

Priority Recommendations

Ranked by effort-to-impact. The first two require no new content at all. With June confirming there is no organic recovery underway, these move from recommended to time-critical.

🚨
1. Fix the BUN Content CTR

BUN/creatinine articles still convert terribly — 0.25% CTR on 297K June impressions (the impressions themselves fell with the May 22 drop). Rewriting title tags to match search intent remains the cheapest click win on the board.

🤝
2. Joint Cannibalization Cleanup

505 self-competing query groups across both streams, ~128K clicks/month at stake. Start with the 13 high-severity groups: merge duplicates, 301 the losers, and adopt a shared keyword map.

🎯
3. Refresh Declining GLP-1 Content

The flagship retatrutide dosage article is down 80% from May, and Google moved its traffic to a competing internal duplicate (+67%). Consolidate the dosage cluster now, freshen with Phase 3 data, and add FAQ schema — the June winner-swap proves the authority is splitting.

🌿
4. Monitor Spring Content

5,802 new articles are indexing. Set an Aug–Sep 2026 benchmark, submit via GSC URL Inspection, and add internal links from ranking articles to the strongest new pieces.

🗑
5. Prune 678 Delete Candidates

678 articles have zero visibility and consume crawl budget. Redirect topical matches to performing pages; return 410 Gone for the rest.

📱
6. Fix /ai-primary-care CTR

This page’s impressions collapsed from 249K to 8.6K in June — it largely fell out of results in the May 22 event. Fold it into the recovery push: re-optimize title/meta for commercial “medical AI” intent and rebuild internal links from ranking pages.
